05: May the laughs be with you! Warmer weather, longer days, and comedians who've finally figured out their material.
06: Summer is coming! Laugh your way into the season before you realize you haven't achieved your New Year's resolutions.
Zürich's June sun refuses to set before 9pm, giving you absolutely no excuse not to come out and laugh.
In a city famous for running on time, comedians remain the only ones cheerfully allowed to go off-script.
The lake is sparkling, the Alps are showing off, and somewhere indoors a comedian is about to absolutely destroy a room.
Zürich summers are short, the punchlines are sharp, and the beer is cold: make the most of all three.
June brings the longest evenings and the shortest distance between strangers laughing in the dark.
The Alps are pretty but they have never once made you snort your drink, and that is what we are here for.
Even the most punctual city in the world cannot tell you exactly when the next big laugh will hit you.
Summer in Zürich means the terraces fill up, the mood lifts, and someone on a microphone ruins a perfectly good silence.
It stays light until ten, the comedians stay later, and your excuses for going home early are officially invalid.
The evenings last forever in June, so you might as well spend one of them laughing at a stranger with a microphone.
Zürich in June: the lake glitters, the terraces are packed, and somewhere tonight a comic is about to bomb magnificently.
Nobody needs a reason to laugh in June, but long evenings, warm air, and live comedy make a very persuasive case anyway.
After months of sensible Swiss winter, June arrives with the radical idea that you stay out past 10pm and laugh loudly at something.
07: The heat is on! Cool down with comedy that's hotter than the Swiss summer sun.
July in Zürich: the sun stays out until ten, the comedians stay on until eleven, and everyone pretends they have work tomorrow.
The city is warm, the beer gardens are full, and somewhere on a small stage someone is about to say the thing you were thinking.
Switzerland may have invented the cuckoo clock, but in July we prefer our comedians to run a little over time.
The sun refuses to go home until ten, so you may as well stay out a little longer and laugh at something.
Switzerland gave the world banks, watches, and neutrality: July is when it quietly adds punchlines to the list.
July loosens the collar of even the most composed Swiss audience, and the comedians know exactly what to do with that.
Every summer the tourists arrive looking for chocolate and mountains, and some of them accidentally find something even better.
There is no better way to spend a warm Zürich evening than sitting in the dark laughing at a stranger's worst decisions.
Consider this your official permission to spend a midsummer night indoors, in the dark, losing your composure completely.
July in Zürich means the sun stays up late and so do the comedians, grab a cold drink and a seat before both run out.
The evenings are long, the lake is warm, and the only thing cooler than a swim right now is the punchline you never saw coming.
Zürich summers draw visitors from everywhere, so come prepared: the locals laugh in multiple languages and always precisely on time.
When July turns Zürich into one giant terrace party, it turns out the funniest table is always indoors.
08: The lake is warm, the evenings are long, and someone is about to say something deeply unwise into a microphone.
Before the Swiss summer packs its bags and the serious season returns, come let strangers make you laugh embarrassingly hard.
Zürich in August: too hot to think, too bright to sleep, and exactly the right temperature for a bad decision at a comedy show.
August in Zürich means warm evenings, cold drinks, and comedians who have had all summer to perfect their excuses.
The Alps are lovely but they never made anyone laugh, that is what this is for.
Summer is almost over, your tan is already fading, so you may as well come inside and laugh about it.
Late summer in Switzerland: the clocks are precise, the fondue is months away, and the punchlines are right on time.
Consider this your cultural contribution to August, minus the hiking boots and the sunscreen.
The days are still long, the nights are finally cool, and someone on stage is about to say exactly what you were thinking.
August in Zürich: too hot to be polite, too humid to be Swiss, and just right for laughing at it all.
The lake is warm, the rosé is cold, and someone up there has forty-five minutes of material about both.
Summer is technically still happening, comedians are definitely still happening, and together you will survive August.
Before the first fondue of autumn shows up, let a stranger make you laugh until your sunburn hurts.
09: The sweaters are out, the tourists are gone, and September in Zürich belongs entirely to people who laugh.
Precision watches, flawless trains, zero tolerance for bad cheese, and now a month of people determined to make you laugh.
September is the universe's polite warning that winter is coming, so you may as well spend it laughing.
Summer packed its bags and left without a word, so we brought in comedians instead.
The leaves are turning gold, the air is getting crisp, and someone on stage is about to make a fool of themselves for your entertainment.
September in Zürich: the city trades sunscreen for scarves and we trade sunshine for punchlines.
Autumn is officially here, which means it is finally acceptable to spend your evenings indoors laughing at strangers.
As the Limmat cools and the chestnut vendors set up shop, the city's comedians are warming up their best material.
September is the month Zürich stops being a postcard and starts being a place where people actually laugh together.
The summer tan fades, the fondue season begins, and Zürich's comedians are ready to fill the gap in between.
September: the month Zürich puts away its sunscreen, pulls out its scarves, and finally has something worth complaining about on stage.
As the leaves start falling and the lake goes grey, nothing warms a room quite like someone else's bad life choices.
Back from holidays, slightly rounder, and full of stories you'd only share with strangers in the dark: welcome to September.
10: The leaves are falling, the fondue is bubbling, and someone in Zürich is about to make a room full of strangers laugh until it hurts.
October in Zürich: the clocks change, the temperature drops, and the only reliable warmth left is a punchline landing exactly on time.
When October turns Zürich grey and staying out feels brave, a good comedian is all the excuse you need to leave the house.
The chestnuts are roasting, the fog is rolling in off the lake, and the comedians are just warming up.
October in Zürich goes golden fast, the evenings get sharp, and the only sensible response is to laugh loudly in a dark room.
The clocks go back this month, which means one extra hour to wonder why you ever stayed home instead of coming to a show.
As the rest of the city retreats into scarves and silence, these stages stay loud, warm, and gloriously unpredictable.
Fondue weather has arrived, the lake is doing its moody thing, and someone with a microphone has a lot of thoughts about all of it.
Stand-up comedy and a cold October night were made for each other, and Zürich keeps proving the point.
The leaves are falling, the laughs are rising: October in Zürich is the perfect time to let a comedian do the talking.
October brings shorter days and longer punchlines, come inside, the fog can wait.
Zürich in October: the chestnuts are roasting, the scarves are out, and the only thing sharper than the autumn air is the comedy.
When the lake turns grey and the clocks go back, the only sensible response is to sit in a dark room and laugh your head off.
11: November in Zürich: dark, damp, and absolutely ideal for laughing at strangers in a heated room.
The leaves are down, the fondue is bubbling, and somebody onstage is about to say something they cannot unsay.
When the fog rolls in off the lake and the days shrink to nothing, stand-up comedy starts to look like a very good idea.
November in Zürich: the fog rolls in for free, the laughs cost a franc or two, and only one of them is worth it.
When the sun clocks out at half past four, gathering in a warm room to laugh is basically survival.
Fondue season and comedy season share the same logic: better when it is hot, better with people around you.
The trees have let go of everything, take the hint, come inside, and leave your bad week at the door.
Zürich in November: cold enough outside that even the most committed homebody deserves a night out.
November is essentially the universe asking whether you can find something funny about November.
November in Zürich means shorter days, longer coats, and the urgent need to sit somewhere warm and laugh at things.
Swiss engineering can handle anything except a grey Tuesday in November, which is where comedians come in.
The lake turns steel, the leaves give up, and the funniest people in the city take it as a personal challenge.
There is no better cure for late autumn than a room full of strangers all deciding, at once, that this is actually funny.
12: The nights are long, the glühwein is warm, and somebody has to make the whole city laugh before the year gives up.
Forget the Christmas markets for one evening and let someone professionally ruin the carol that has been stuck in your head since November.
December in Zürich is breathtaking, borderline unaffordable, and absolutely overflowing with material for anyone holding a microphone.
December in Zürich is cold enough to freeze a punchline mid-air, so come inside and let the laughter do the heating.
Skip the third Glühwein and spend an evening laughing instead, your liver and your sides will both thank you.
The Swiss make everything on time, including the punchline: December comedy in Zürich, reliably funnier than your office party.
When the sun clocks off at four in the afternoon, the least you can do is show up somewhere with good lighting and better jokes.
Before the relatives arrive with their opinions, treat yourself to professionals who actually know how to deliver a good line.
December is the one month everyone needs to laugh at themselves, and Zürich has just the people to help you do it.
Nothing cuts through a Zürich December like shared laughter, warmer than fondue and significantly cheaper than Swiss chocolate.
This festive season, the best gift you can give yourself is an evening spent laughing until your Glühwein goes cold.
December in Zürich means snow on the rooftops, lights on the bridges, and comedians who refuse to let winter have the last word.
The nights are long, the cheese is melted, and the comedians are ready: Zürich's December stand-up scene is officially open for business.
